text: After placing the ports, the surgeon observes the gallbladder and immediately inserts a suction irrigator, avoiding the use of a grasper for retraction. The ports are positioned with a Hassan technique at the umbilicus, allowing the hook to be introduced through this entry point.
The surgeon positions the camera at the right midclavicular line for a clear view of the surgical field during the robotic cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses a fenestrated bipolar instrument in the left hand, approaching the surgical field at the right anterior axillary line.
The surgeon positions the fourth robotic port at the left midclavicular line, through which the suction irrigator is introduced into the surgical field.
The surgeon uses the section irrigator as a retractor, pushing the liver or gallbladder upwards to improve visibility from the current angle.
The camera view reveals a notably elongated gallbladder, positioned for further surgical manipulation.
Using robotic instruments, the surgeon retracts the gallbladder to expose Calot's triangle, providing a clear view of the anatomical structures within this area.
The surgeon maintains an open Calot's triangle, facilitating easy dissection around the cystic duct and artery, with clear visibility of the anatomical structures.
The surgeon activates the Firefly imaging system, revealing the common hepatic duct and common bile duct as vertical green structures within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses the Firefly imaging system to map the dissection path, highlighting critical structures such as the common hepatic duct and common bile duct with green fluorescence.
The surgeon removes superficial peritoneal tissue using robotic instruments, revealing underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon uses the Firefly imaging system to identify green-highlighted structures, indicating the location of the common hepatic duct and common bile duct within the surgical field.
The surgeon observes green structures using the Firefly imaging system, speculating that one may be the cystic duct, although the exact identification of these structures remains uncertain.
The surgeon identifies a structure that is likely the common hepatic duct, visible through the robotic surgical system.
The surgeon visually identifies the common hepatic duct positioned in close proximity to the gallbladder, highlighting the anatomical relationship between these structures.
The surgeon encounters a fibrotic area during dissection near the gallbladder, indicating a potentially unsafe region for further dissection.
The surgeon attempts to hook the fibrotic tissue near the gallbladder, but the tissue does not engage well with the hook instrument.

The Firefly fluorescence imaging system illuminates the bile ducts in bright green, enhancing visibility and reducing the likelihood of missing a bile duct during the dissection of the triangle of Calot.
The Firefly imaging system fails to illuminate the targeted area with the indocyanine green (ICG) administered pre-operatively, indicating a lack of fluorescence in the tissue.

The surgeon employs a dome-down approach to detach the gallbladder from the liver bed, facilitating its retraction away from the liver. This maneuver aids in maintaining a safe distance from critical structures during dissection in the triangle of Calot.
The surgeon uses a suction irrigator as a retractor, which occasionally slips off but effectively aids in maintaining exposure of the surgical field.
The surgeon has entered the gallbladder and uses a suction device to evacuate the clear hydropic fluid from within.
The surgeon utilizes a combination of a suction irrigator and hook inside the abdomen, enhancing the efficiency of the operation by facilitating fluid management and tissue manipulation.
The surgeon activates the Firefly imaging system to enhance visualization of the liver, highlighting its location within the surgical field.
The surgeon activates the suction device to evacuate smoke from the surgical field while simultaneously using the instrument for tissue retraction.
The Firefly imaging system highlights the junction between the liver and gallbladder, providing visual guidance for the surgeon.
The surgeon utilizes the suction irrigator as a multifunctional tool, employing it for dissection, suction, smoke evacuation, and locating a bleeding vessel within the surgical field.
The surgeon activates the Firefly imaging system to check for bile leaks after cutting into the liver, ensuring no small bile ducts are injured during the procedure.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the gallbladder, detaching it from the liver bed.
The surgeon uses the robotic system to visualize inside the lumen, allowing them to maintain proximity to the gallbladder during the procedure.
The surgeon approaches the critical structures near the hepatocystic triangle while dissecting the gallbladder.
The surgeon maneuvers the robotic instruments to closely follow the contour of the gallbladder, maintaining proximity to its surface during dissection.

The surgeon assesses the difficulty of separating the gallbladder from the liver bed and decides to abandon dissection of the back wall, focusing instead on removing the front portion of the gallbladder.
The surgeon cauterizes the mucosa of the gallbladder, revealing the cystic duct opening from the inside. A 3-0 PDS or Vicryl suture is used to close off the cystic duct.
The robotic camera provides a view similar to traditional laparoscopic techniques, showing the gallbladder and surrounding anatomy.
The surgeon uses a hook as a retractor to lift the liver while employing a suction irrigator, providing a clear view of the surgical field.
The surgeon continues to remove the remaining portion of the gallbladder, which is still attached to the liver bed.
The surgeon operates on the lateral side of the gallbladder, ensuring safety while manipulating the tissue in this area.
The surgeon manipulates the gallbladder, revealing a noticeable bulge indicative of a large gallstone within the organ.

The surgeon uses a suction irrigator to remove smoke from the surgical field, attempting to retract the gallbladder despite the presence of a large gallstone. The Firefly imaging system is employed to visualize the junction between the liver and gallbladder.

The surgeon navigates around the posterior aspect of the gallbladder, cautiously approaching the area near the common hepatic duct, right hepatic duct, and hepatic artery, ensuring not to extend dissection too far in this direction.
The gallbladder visibly falls away from the liver, improving exposure of the surgical field and allowing clearer visualization of the anatomical structures.
The surgeon navigates near the hepatic artery or right hepatic artery, with the anatomical structures becoming visible soon.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers the instruments to closely follow the contour of the gallbladder, ensuring precise dissection while maintaining proximity to the gallbladder surface.
Using the Firefly imaging system, the surgeon visualizes the duct, which is highlighted for better identification during the robotic cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon navigates the robotic instruments, veering slightly away from the gallbladder, which is visibly separated from the liver, providing clear exposure of the surgical field.
The surgeon encounters fibrotic tissue, making it difficult to visually distinguish between the gallbladder and adjacent liver tissue.
The surgeon uses their left hand to maintain control of a bleeding structure, ensuring it remains intact during the procedure.
The surgeon uses a section irrigator to manipulate and push surrounding tissues away, followed by cauterization to control bleeding or separate tissue.
The surgeon identifies the cystic artery, visible amidst fibrotic tissue, using robotic instruments to carefully distinguish it from surrounding liver tissue.
The surgeon addresses the back wall of the gallbladder, which remains attached to an adjacent structure, using robotic instruments.
The surgeon attempts to dissect the entire gallbladder, focusing on isolating the cystic duct and cystic artery from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon uses a suction device to clean the surgical field, ensuring clear visibility and avoiding the use of bipolar cautery on inappropriate areas.
The surgeon identifies the putative cystic artery, distinguishing it from the cystic duct during the dissection process.
The surgeon attempts to skeletonize the cystic duct and cystic artery, carefully removing surrounding tissue to expose these structures.
The surgeon observes the structure, increasingly resembling an artery, likely the cystic artery, as dissection progresses.
The surgeon identifies the cystic duct positioned low in the surgical field, preparing for further dissection.
The surgeon identifies a structure as most likely the cystic artery and administers indocyanine green (ICG) dye to confirm its identity visually.
The surgeon requests the administration of two milliliters of indocyanine green (ICG) to enhance visualization of the suspected cystic artery.
The surgeon visually identifies a structure that appears to be the cystic artery, with surrounding tissue being carefully skeletonized.
The surgeon observes the cystic artery lighting up immediately after the administration of ICG, confirming its identification amidst the scarred and inflamed tissue.
The gallbladder is positioned far from the hepatocystic triangle, facilitating easier dissection by maintaining distance from the common hepatic duct and right hepatic artery.
The robotic instruments maneuver closely around the gallbladder, maintaining proximity to its surface during dissection.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers the robotic instruments to maintain close proximity to the gallbladder, ensuring precise dissection while remaining distant from critical structures.
The surgeon carefully maintains close proximity to the gallbladder during dissection, ensuring not to veer away from the targeted anatomical structure.
The surgeon observes a distinct, thickened outer layer or 'rind' on the gallbladder, indicating its separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon identifies the cystic artery, which is visible near the gallbladder, ensuring careful dissection to avoid nearby structures.
The surgeon begins to achieve the critical view of safety, ensuring clear identification of the cystic duct and artery while maintaining close proximity to the gallbladder.
The surgeon observes a significant amount of tissue surrounding the cystic duct, indicating the need for further dissection to clearly expose the duct.
The anatomy of the gallbladder region becomes more distinct, revealing clearer delineation of the cystic duct and artery as the dissection progresses.
The surgeon identifies the cystic artery, which is clearly visible in the surgical field.
The cystic duct is now clearly skeletonized, with surrounding tissue meticulously dissected away to reveal its structure.
The surgeon prepares to divide the cystic artery to better isolate the cystic duct, ensuring a clear view for placing a clip on the duct.

The surgeon waits for the administration of ICG to visualize the cystic artery. The hepatic artery lights up first, followed by the cystic artery, revealing the proximity of these arterial structures to the dissection site.
The surgeon manipulates the gallbladder, hugging it closely to isolate the cystic duct and artery for further dissection.
The surgeon clips the cystic artery on the patient's side and uses a hook to divide the artery on the specimen side, with bipolar cautery ready if bleeding occurs.
The surgeon uses the Firefly imaging system to guide the skeletonization of the cystic artery, enhancing visualization of the arterial structure for precise dissection.
The surgeon opts to skeletonize the cystic artery and apply clips across it instead of using a stapler.
The surgeon observes a structure that visually resembles a vein, assessing its characteristics within the surgical field.
The surgeon applies a clip between the cystic duct and an adjacent structure, utilizing a small window for precise placement.
The surgeon uses the Firefly imaging system to check for any aberrant ducts branching from the cystic duct, ensuring clear visualization of the anatomical structures.
The surgeon uses the Firefly imaging system to delineate the cystic duct, aiming to achieve greater length for further manipulation.
The surgeon continues dissection in Firefly mode, which enhances visualization of the cystic duct and surrounding structures, aiding in the identification and separation of anatomical features.
The surgeon uses ICG (Indocyanine Green) imaging to identify the artery, confirming the absence of arterial structures adjacent to the cystic duct, ensuring a safe dissection area.
The common hepatic duct is visible on the right side of the surgical field, with the cystic duct joining it at a point to the right.
The surgeon uses Firefly imaging to visualize and assess the length of the cystic duct, which is clearly delineated against surrounding structures.
The surgeon gains additional length on the cystic duct, preparing to apply clips for secure closure.
The surgeon places clips on the cystic duct, ensuring they are securely attached without being too tenuous.
The surgeon's suction device is actively cleaning the surgical field while awaiting the assistant to switch the instrument to clips.
The surgeon uses the Firefly imaging system to verify the anatomical structures, ensuring consistency with the view seen under white light.
The surgeon inspects the liver bed for any visible bile leaks, ensuring the area is secure and free from leakage.
The surgeon uses electrocautery to address the area between the clips, ensuring precise tissue manipulation and hemostasis.
The surgeon uses a hook to divide tissue, avoiding the need to open scissors for this part of the robotic cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon replaces the hook instrument with an EndoCatch device, preparing to capture and remove tissue or specimens from the surgical field.
The surgeon positions the endocatch device through the umbilical Hassan port, highlighting the strategic placement of the port for efficient retrieval.
The specimen is positioned within the umbilical port, ready for extraction using the endocatch device.
The surgeon enlarges the fascia and skin to accommodate the removal of a larger stone.
The surgeon enlarges the fascia and skin at the umbilicus, facilitating easier access for specimen retrieval compared to off midline approaches.
